← Back to home

Effective Actions

A brief overview of my work on supersymmetry, supergravity, and the low-energy effective action of M-theory, adapted from NSF proposal material.

The broad goal is to understand how a fundamental theory such as string/M-theory gives rise, at low energies, to the spacetime physics we actually observe. In practice, this means asking whether symmetry—especially supersymmetry—can be strong enough to reconstruct an effective action containing infinitely many higher-derivative corrections.

Why effective actions?

A complete quantum theory of gravity should ultimately explain such questions as the origin of spacetime, the fate of singularities, and the physics of the early universe. We do not yet have a background-independent, directly usable formulation of M-theory that answers these questions in a transparent way. A more practical route is therefore to understand its low-energy effective action: the organized expansion that encodes the leading corrections to ordinary supergravity.

The model example is Born-Infeld theory. In open-string theory, the effective dynamics of an abelian gauge field are governed by the Born-Infeld action, a non-linear extension of Maxwell theory. That result suggests a broader strategy: rather than deriving every correction term directly from first principles, one can try to determine the effective action from a combination of symmetry, supersymmetry, and consistency conditions.

Supersymmetry as a reconstruction principle

One of the central lessons of supersymmetric field theory is that the right combination of manifest and hidden supersymmetries can be astonishingly restrictive. In four dimensions, the work of Bagger and Galperin showed that the Born-Infeld action is singled out by a partial breaking of extended supersymmetry. Put differently: start from supersymmetric Maxwell theory, deform it by the leading higher-derivative term, and require invariance under an additional non-manifest supersymmetry. The result is not an arbitrary deformation, but the full Born-Infeld theory.

My work asks whether a similar mechanism can be used in supergravity and M-theory. If so, then supersymmetry would not merely constrain the effective action; it could provide a practical method for reconstructing it.

11D supergravity and the M-theory effective action

The most immediate target is the leading low-energy effective action of M-theory, which is expected to begin with higher-derivative corrections built from curvature, especially terms schematically of the form (Riemann)4. The difficulty is that there is no known fully off-shell superspace preserving the full eleven-dimensional super-Poincaré symmetry in a form suitable for this construction.

To get around this, I have worked on an eleven-dimensional off-shell superspace with reduced structure group SL(2, C) × G2. In this framework, four supersymmetries are manifest and off-shell, while the remaining twenty-eight are realized linearly but close only on-shell. This allows the full set of constraints to be solved in terms of a finite set of eleven-dimensional superfields. The hope is that this is the right setting in which to construct the (Riemann)4 deformation and then determine how much of the full infinite higher-derivative series it generates.

A key technical issue is the auxiliary-field sector. Once the deformation is introduced, the auxiliary fields must be re-diagonalized or integrated out. That step is expected to generate an infinite tower of additional terms and is therefore one of the main places where the effective action becomes genuinely non-trivial.

Simpler laboratories: Born-Infeld and chiral 2-forms

Because the auxiliary-field problem may be difficult to solve directly in eleven dimensions, part of the program is to study simpler non-polynomial theories that appear to realize the same mechanisms in a more manageable form.

  • Ten-dimensional super-Born-Infeld theory. The goal here is to construct the 10D, N = 1 supersymmetric Born-Infeld action in a reduced superspace with only four manifest supercharges. In lower-dimensional analogues this structure can already be seen, but in ten dimensions additional representation-theoretic complications appear that are likely to illuminate the corresponding eleven-dimensional problem.
  • The self-interacting chiral 2-form in six dimensions. The Perry-Schwarz model provides a non-linear theory for a self-dual 2-form, one of the key ingredients of the M5-brane worldvolume theory. This system is the tensor-field analogue of Born-Infeld theory and may offer a cleaner setting in which to understand how reduced structure groups, hidden symmetries, and non-linear effective actions fit together.

Longer-term aim

The longer-term aim is to understand whether these reduced-superspace constructions can reveal a general mechanism by which the low-energy effective action of M-theory is determined. Even partial success would be valuable: obtaining controlled sectors of the effective action, clarifying the role of auxiliary fields, and understanding how hidden supersymmetries organize the infinite higher-derivative expansion would all sharpen our picture of quantum gravity.

Selected references

  1. R. R. Metsaev, Malik Rakhmanov, and Arkady A. Tseytlin, The Born-Infeld Action as the Effective Action in the Open Superstring Theory, Phys. Lett. B193:207–212 (1987).
  2. M. Born and L. Infeld, Foundations of the new field theory, Nature 132(3348):1004.1 (1933).
  3. J. Bagger and A. Galperin, A New Goldstone multiplet for partially broken supersymmetry, Phys. Rev. D55:1091–1098 (1997), arXiv:hep-th/9608177.
  4. S. Cecotti and S. Ferrara, Supersymmetric Born-Infeld Lagrangians, Phys. Lett. B187:335–339 (1987).
  5. Katrin Becker, Melanie Becker, Daniel Butter, William D. Linch, and Stephen Randall, Five-dimensional Supergravity in N = 1/2 Superspace, JHEP 03:098 (2020), arXiv:1909.09208.
  6. Malcolm Perry and John H. Schwarz, Interacting chiral gauge fields in six-dimensions and Born-Infeld theory, Nucl. Phys. B489:47–64 (1997), hep-th/9611065.
  7. Neil Marcus, Augusto Sagnotti, and Warren Siegel, Ten-dimensional Supersymmetric Yang-Mills Theory in Terms of Four-dimensional Superfields, Nucl. Phys. B224:159 (1983), INSPIRE entry.
  8. Nima Arkani-Hamed, Thomas Gregoire, and Jay G. Wacker, Higher dimensional supersymmetry in 4-D superspace, JHEP 03:055 (2002), hep-th/0101233.
